title: Analysis of Volume Holographic Gratings With Statistical Distribution
paper_keyword: Volume Holographic Grating (VHG), Finite Element Method (FEM), Diffraction Characteristics, Refractive Index Modulation, Fourier Expansion
paper_text:
ABSTRACT
For simulating the actual diffraction characteristics of the volume holographic grating (VHG), this paper incorporates multiple periodic gratings into the finite element method (FEM) analysis. The grating periods and refractive index modulation are modeled according to statistical distribution. Thus, the refractive index distribution within the gratings is reconstructed through the Fourier expansion. Utilizing the enhanced model, a quantitative analysis was performed to examine the relationships among model parameters, in relation to the diffraction characteristics. The results demonstrated that the optimized model achieves a correlation degree R 2 of 98% with experimental data. This model also provided a reliable theoretical basis for the precise design of optical systems based on VHGs.
author: Zhongwen Shen, Chao Ping Chen, Zhendong Wu, Yan Yang, Dawei Zhu

title: Three hundred sixty-degree viewable linear grooves 3D display
paper_keyword: 3D image, arc 3D display, conical display, curved surface
paper_text:
Abstract
We proposed a linear grooves 3D display that can display 360° viewable 3D images by creating linear grooves that correspond to the viewpoint positions. In the conventional method, arc-shaped grooves are drawn on a flat substrate of transparent film. However, depending on the viewing position, the image may be distorted. In addition, when a flat substrate is bent into a curved shape, the image is more affected than one observed in a flat shape. Furthermore, the conventional method of arc 3D display method cannot display a 3D image that corresponds to the viewpoint position. In this paper, we propose design and fabrication methods that reduce the image distortion for both flat and curved shapes. A 3D image displayed by the proposed method can change depending on the viewpoint position, and a natural 3D image with motion parallax can be observed. In the experiment, our proposed method fabricated a 360° 3D display by calculating the linear grooves and presented natural 3D images with smooth motion parallax.

title: Highly efficient green-emissive organic light emitting diodes based on multiple-resonance boron-nitrogen materials
paper_text:
Abstract
Green-emission phosphor-sensitized OLEDs were demonstrated to achieve extremely high performance. SY-FGD8-based sensitized top-emission devices achieved current efficiency of 232.8 cd/A@10 mA/cm2, LT95@50 mA/cm2, room temperature of 71 h and LT80@20 mA/cm2, 85°C of 187 h, which are greatly improved by 28%, 29%, and 65%, respectively, compared to phosphorescent devices. Notably, SY-FGD8 exhibited excellent compatibility with different host and phosphorescent sensitizers.

title: Short range optical communication with GaN-on-Si microLED and microPD matrices
paper_keyword: characterization, communication FDTD, GaN, microLED, microPD, TCAD, VLC
paper_text:
Abstract
(In)GaN microLEDs have reached a high degree of maturity due to their development in the lighting industry. Their robustness and high efficiency make them ideal candidates for high-brightness, high-resolution micro-displays. Beyond display applications, microLEDs are being explored for non-display uses, including wireless Visible Light Communication (VLC) and parallel communication via multicore fiber. This study investigates short-range chip-to-chip optical communication using InGaN/GaN microLEDs and micro Photodiodes (microPDs). Leveraging processes developed for micro-displays, we address the challenges of integrating GaN microLEDs and microPDs on ASICs. We outline the main figures of merit, including expected energy efficiency, optical coupling to multicore fibers or waveguides, and the spectral efficiency of InGaN/GaN microPDs correlated with TCAD simulation and experimental transmission results. Our study highlights the potential of GaN microLEDs and microPDs for massively parallel, energy-efficient data transmission, paving the way for innovative short-range and energy-efficient optical communication solutions.

title: High-speed and contactless inspection of defective micro-LEDs through the photovoltaic effect
paper_keyword: detection, EPIS, inspection, micro-LED, photovoltaic effect, proximity capacitance imagesensor
paper_text:
Abstract
The most significant challenge associated with micro-light-emitting-diode (micro-LED) displays, which are anticipated to be the next generation of display technology, is the high manufacturing cost. In order to reduce manufacturing costs, it is essential to improve yield. Improving the manufacturing yield of them necessitates the evaluation of micro-LED chips prior to their installation onto substrates. However, the microsize and large quantity of these chips renders inspection difficult with conventional inspection methods. Herein, we propose a method for inspecting micro-LED chips by measuring the voltage generated between the anode and cathode due to the photovoltaic effect using a developed proximity capacitance image sensor. As this inspection method does not require the use of probe pins to contact LED electrodes, it enables simultaneous inspection of multiple chips in a short time without causing any damage to the electrodes. In this paper, an experimental system equipped with this sensor was developed to demonstrate the basic measurement principle. Moreover, we demonstrated that more than 50,000 micro-LED chips with a size of 60 μm × 34 μm can be simultaneously inspected in approximately 2 s.

1 INTRODUCTION
Currently, most flat panel displays (FPDs) for televisions, smartphones, tablets, personal computer monitors, and other devices utilize either liquid crystal displays (LCDs) or organic light-emitting diode (OLED) displays.1 LCDs are generally less expensive to manufacture than OLED displays. However, they have a narrow dynamic range, slow response, and poor energy efficiency because they are not self-emissive.2, 3 Meanwhile, OLED displays, being self-emissive, boast a wide dynamic range and fast response. However, organic materials used in OLEDs exhibit durability issues due to heat, such as screen burn-in and degradation of image quality. Consequently, it is not possible to enhance the luminance by increasing the current.4, 5

Micro-light-emitting-diode (micro-LED) displays are anticipated to be the next generation of FPDs.6-9 This display technology controls the emission of a huge number of LED chips each less than 100 μm in size mounted on a substrate. These displays are self-emissive, boasting a wide dynamic range, fast response, wide color gamut, and low power consumption due to their high luminous efficiency. Furthermore, since they comprise inorganic materials, increasing the current for high-luminance usage poses no issues. Additionally, micro-LEDs can realize displays of over 2000ppi, which is difficult to achieve with LCDs and OLEDs. Therefore, micro-LEDs are expected to be used as light engines for augmented reality and virtual reality, which require high resolution.10, 11 The biggest challenge for mass production of micro-LED displays is the manufacturing cost, and it is essential to increase the yield to reduce the manufacturing cost. To enhance the yields, it is crucial to assess the quality of products obtained through each manufacturing process. In particular, since time is required to replace the already mounted micro-LED chips on substrates, it is preferable to evaluate the chips in advance and mount only those that pass the quality check.

Currently, micro-LED chips undergo inspection through several methods such as inspection of electrical and luminescence characteristics with probe pins making contact with the electrodes,12, 13 optical image inspection,14, 15 and photoluminescence measurements of the chips.16 Inspection methods involving contact between probe pins and electrodes are highly reliable as they allow for the actual measurement of luminescence and electrical characteristics. However, this method is long time time-consuming due to the need to probe numerous electrodes individually. Moreover, pin probing damages the electrodes, and some electrodes are considerably small, rendering probing difficult. Although optical image inspection is a noncontact and high-speed method, it cannot inspect electrical characteristics and tends to over-detect due to particles. Similarly, while the measurement of LED photoluminescence is also a noncontact and high-speed method, it is fundamentally incapable of detecting disconnection in devices.

This paper proposes a method for inspecting micro-LED chips using an electrical picture inspection system (EPIS) equipped with a developed proximity capacitance image sensor.17-20 In the inspection of solar cells based on the photovoltaic effect, an electric current is applied to the solar cell and the resulting electroluminescence is measured.21, 22 However, this method measures the voltage difference generated between the anode and cathode due to the photovoltaic effect when micro-LED chips are exposed to light with wavelengths shorter than their emission wavelengths. EPIS can measure this voltage difference via capacitance without electrical contact. This method allows the simultaneous high-speed inspection of many micro-LED chips, with minimal damage to the electrodes as it eliminates the need for pin probing. Moreover, the high spatial resolution of the sensor allows the inspection of small micro-LED chips. It also enables the examination of the internal connections of devices for disconnections and short circuits. The subsequent sections present the measurement principle, experimental method, and demonstrations using surface-mounted LEDs and actual micro-LEDs.

2 EXPERIMENTAL
2.1 Measurement principle
Figure 1 presents the current–voltage (I–V) curves of a red LED (wavelength: 631 nm) measured with and without light irradiation (wavelength: 630 nm). When the LED is exposed to light with a wavelength shorter than its emission wavelength, carriers are generated via photoexcitation, leading to the flow of a photoelectric current (ISC). Consequently, the I–V curve shifts in the direction of the negative current. The I–V curve can be expressed by the following equation:

Here, I0 represents the reverse saturation current, e represents the elementary charge, n is the ideality factor, k is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature, and V is the bias voltage. When the voltage at zero current is VOC, equation (1) can be transformed into equation (2) as follows:
 
 
(2)
Utilizing VOC, we can evaluate the electrical characteristics of LEDs. Here, I = 0 signifies that the bias voltage is balanced by the voltage generated between the LED electrodes due to the photovoltaic effect. In other words, VOC corresponds to the voltage difference between the LED electrodes due to the photovoltaic effect under open-circuit conditions. Naturally, by measuring this voltage difference, we can detect disconnection and short circuit defects in the device. If the wiring is disconnected, VOC will not appear between the electrodes, and if there is a short circuit, VOC will be zero. Thus, the device can be judged defective.

2.2 Experimental system
Figure 2 presents a photograph and block diagram of the experimental system using EPIS. In this system, the developed proximity capacitance image sensor can be manually positioned above the LEDs to be measured, which are placed on a glass stage. The sensor measures the voltage between the LED electrodes while the LEDs are irradiated with light synchronized to the system from below the glass stage. The sensor's analog output signal is converted into a digital signal by an analog-to-digital converter and transmitted to a PC via serial communication. The angle between the sensor surface and the target surface can be adjusted using a goniometer stage. Moreover, when the sensor is pressed against the target, the sensor surface adheres to the target due to the spring structure of the sensor head. The light source employs high-luminance LEDs with wavelengths of 630 (red), 530 (green), and 450 nm (blue), which can be switched on or off. The luminance of the light source can be regulated by the current ILS flowing through the source LEDs; a linear relationship exists between luminance and ILS. The stage is made of soda lime glass with a thickness of 3 mm, which can transmit light from the aforementioned sources.

2.3 Proximity capacitance image sensor
Figure 3 presents micrographs of the entire sensor chip and an enlarged pixel section of the developed proximity capacitance image sensor. This sensor chip was fabricated by a 0.18 μm 1-poly-Si 5-metal CMOS process. This sensor is a two-dimensional area sensor based on the principle of capacitance, with finely arranged detection electrodes in a matrix of 1,080 pixel × 1,080 pixel and a 12 μm pitch. Each detection electrode is surrounded by guard rings at a ground potential. The voltage distribution of the LED electrodes can be measured through the capacitance between each detection electrode and the LED electrodes by positioning this sensor in close proximity to the LED electrodes. Figure 4a–c presents a cross-sectional schematic showing the measurement principle, a simplified sensor circuit diagram, and the operation timing of the proximity capacitance image sensor, respectively. The capacitance between the sensor's detection electrode and the measured LED electrode is CS, while the internal capacitance of the sensor is CC. Each pixel measures the voltage of the LED electrodes (VL) at the floating node between CS and CC. For this experiment, we synchronized the switching of irradiation with the high and low timing of ΦC. The low-frequency noise and fixed-pattern noise were eliminated by differencing the output voltage when the light was irradiated (VOUTN) and when the light was not irradiated (VOUTS). The time delay between switching off the light source and sampling was defined as tS. Equation (3) provides the output voltage of each pixel (VOUT).

3 RESULTS AND DISCUSSIONS
3.1 Measurement demonstration using surface-mounted LEDs
Initially, to validate the measurement principle of this method, experiments were conducted using surface-mounted LEDs with a size of 2 mm × 1.25 mm. These LEDs are easily accessible when making contact between the pin probes and electrodes. The following LEDs manufactured by LITEON Technology were used in the experiments: red LED (LTST-C170KRKT; wavelength: 631 nm), green LED (LTST-C170KGKT; wavelength: 571 nm), and blue LED (LTST-C170TBKT; wavelength: 470 nm). Figure 6 shows the electrical characteristics evaluated by contacting the electrodes of each LED with the pin probes while the LEDs were exposed to light with wavelengths shorter than their emission wavelengths. Figure 6a–c shows the I–V curves for each LED measured when ILS was varied from 0 to 500 mA. For all LEDs, the photoelectric current ISC increased as ILS increased, causing the I–V curves to shift in the negative current direction. The shift in the case of the blue LED was smaller than those in the case of the red and green LEDs. We consider that this is because when blue light is irradiated onto a blue LED, the light reflection is larger than that of a red or green LED due to the refractive index, resulting in a smaller effective light intensity.23 In other words, we consider that a stronger light is needed to shift the blue LED in the same way as the red and green LEDs. Figure 6d–f shows the results of VOC obtained from the I–V curves (red line) and that measured between LED electrodes under open-circuit conditions using a voltmeter (blue line) when ILS was varied from 0 to 500 mA. The two results aligned for each LED when ILS was 300 mA or higher. Thus, under irradiation with sufficient luminance, VOC can be measured using a voltmeter under open-circuit conditions. As previously stated, because the effective light intensity is small for blue LED, the output impedance of blue LED is considered to be larger than that of red or green LEDs. Consequently, we consider that the VOC of the blue LED at ILS lower than 300 mA appears small due to the ratio between its output impedance and the 10 MΩ internal resistance of the voltmeter.

Figure 7 shows the EPIS measurement images of the voltage distribution in the case of the LED electrodes under red-, green-, and blue-light irradiation for each LED. Measurements were performed at ILS = 300 mA and tS = 100 μs with the sensor surface in contact with the LED electrodes. All LEDs responded solely to light with wavelengths shorter than their emission wavelengths. Due to the photovoltaic effect, the cathode appeared black because of a lower voltage while the anode appeared white because of a higher voltage. The non-uniform color tone of the electrode area can be attributed to the poor flatness of the LED electrodes. The poor contrast under blue-light irradiation compared to those under red- and green-light irradiation could be a result of reflection and absorption effects.

Figure 10 shows a schematic of the EPIS measurement with a 1 MΩ resistor inserted between the electrodes of the red LED, and VOC determined by the EPIS (with and without the resistor) and pin probing measurements. The EPIS measurements were performed at ILS = 300 mA and tS = 100 μs with the sensor surface in contact with the LED electrodes. Consequently, compared to VOC obtained by the EPIS measurements without the resistor (red line), that obtained by the EPIS measurements with the resistor (red-dotted line) increased and was consistent with VOC determined by the pin probing measurements (blue line). As shown in Figure 11, the voltage between the LED electrodes tends to zero when the light source switches from on to off during the EPIS measurement. When the resistor is not inserted, the voltage between the LED electrodes takes time to reach zero because carrier disappearance is solely due to the recombination of electrons and holes inside the LED (red line). In contrast, when the resistor is inserted, the voltage between the LED electrodes drops to zero faster than without the resistor because current flows through the resistor (blue line). Therefore, as the voltage between the LED electrodes determined by the EPIS measurement with resistor reaches zero at tS = 100 μs, VOC determined by the EPIS and pin probing measurements are consistent. Such transient issues do not occur during the pin probing measurement because the voltmeter has an internal resistance of 10 MΩ and is a DC measurement device, which allows sufficient time to saturate VOC. In other words, this measurement method can also measure the recombination rates of electrons and holes in LEDs, which cannot be detected by DC measurements. Figure 12 illustrates the relationship between VOC determined by the EPIS (without the resistor) and pin probing measurements for the red LED, as obtained from the results of Figure 10b. During an actual inspection of the micro-LED chips by the EPIS measurements, VOC can be determined without inserting a resistor by measuring at a fixed sampling timing and calibrating the measured value using such a correction curve. Moreover, even if correction curves cannot be obtained because pin probing is not possible, the micro-LED chips can be evaluated by comparing the measured values with those of the surrounding chips.

3.2 Measurement demonstration using micro-LED chips
Figure 13 presents a schematic of red micro-LED chips (wavelength: 630 nm), each with a size of 60 μm × 34 μm, formed in large numbers on a sapphire wafer; their EPIS measurement images; and VOC determined by the EPIS measurements for each chip. Measurements were performed with the sensor surface in contact with the LED electrodes under green-light irradiation at ILS = 300 mA and tS = 100 μs. The EPIS measurement image was averaged for 10 measurement image data for noise reduction. Despite performing 10 measurements, more than 50,000 micro-LED chips could be simultaneously inspected in approximately 2 s because the sensor size was 12.96 mm × 12.96 mm. The cathode of each chip appeared black because of a lower voltage while the anode appeared white due to a higher voltage. Furthermore, the chip at location E3 clearly showed a higher contrast between the anode and cathode than the other chips and the obtained VOC value was about twice as high as those for the other chips. Such LED electrical characteristics cannot be detected by optical inspection. Moreover, it is difficult to make contact with small micro-LED electrodes using pin probes. Further, even if it is possible to make a contact, inspecting all chips through the pin probing measurement is time-consuming. However, it is demonstrated herein that the EPIS measurements can detect the electrical characteristics of numerous chips and perform simultaneous high-speed inspections without pin probing, even for micro-LED chips.

4 CONCLUSION
Herein, we propose a method for evaluating LEDs based on the voltage between their electrodes, which results from the photovoltaic effect, using a developed proximity capacitance image sensor. This method can be measured at high speeds without any electrical contact. Moreover, the electrical characteristics of more than 50,000 micro-LED chips with a size of 60 μm × 34 μm could be measured simultaneously in approximately 2 s without pin probing. Although micro-LED chips will become smaller in the future, they can be inspected by using sensors with even smaller pixel pitches in principle. It is difficult to accurately inspect such microsize and large quantities of micro-LED chips at high speed with conventional inspection methods. This inspection method can improve the manufacturing yield of micro-LED displays by assessing the quality of micro-LED chips before mounting them on substrates. Consequently, we believe that it can reduce manufacturing costs and contribute to the mass production of micro-LED displays.

title: Wide-viewing-angle dual-view integral imaging display
paper_text:
Abstract
A wide-viewing-angle dual-view integral imaging display is proposed. A micro-lens array, a polarizer parallax barrier, and a display panel are aligned in sequence. The display panel is covered with the polarizer parallax barrier. Two types of orthogonal polarizer slits in the polarizer parallax barrier are alternately aligned. Two types of orthogonal polarizer slits polarize the lights from two types of elemental images. The micro-lens array propagates two types of polarized lights into two primary viewing zones, which coincide at the optimal viewing distance. Different 3D images are, respectively, observed through two types of polarizer glasses. The viewing angle is enhanced and unrelated to the number of elemental images.

title: Scene-content-sensitive real-time adaptive foveated rendering
paper_keyword: foveated rendering, variable rate shading, visual perception
paper_text:
Abstract
In recent years, techniques for accelerating rendering by exploiting the limitations of the human visual system have become increasingly prevalent. The foveated rendering method significantly reduces the computational requirements during rendering by reducing image quality in peripheral regions. In this paper, we propose a scene-content-sensitive real-time adaptive foveated rendering method. First, we pre-render the three-dimensional (3D) scene at a low resolution. Then, we utilize the low-resolution pre-rendered image as input to extract edge, local contrast, and color features. Subsequently, we generate a screen-space region division map based on the gaze point position. Next, we calculate the visual importance of each 16 × 16 pixel tile based on edge, local contrast, color, and screen-space region. We then map the visual importance to the shading rate to generate a shading rate control map for the current frame. Finally, we complete the rendering of the current frame based on variable rate shading technology. Experimental results demonstrate that our method effectively enhances the visual quality of images near the foveal region while generating high quality foveal region images. Furthermore, our method can significantly improve performance compared to per-pixel shading method and existing scene-content-based foveated rendering methods.

title: A new gate driver employing linearly sweep signal generated module for high-resolution micro-LED display with line-by-line pulse width modulation driving method
paper_keyword: analog PWM driving, gate driver, line-by-line driving method, metal oxide (MO), micro-light-emitting diode (micro-LED), thin-film transistor (TFT)
paper_text:
Abstract
A new gate driver with metal oxide thin-film transistors (MO-TFTs) has been presented for micro-light-emitting diode (Micro-LED) display. The proposed gate driver employing linearly decreasing sweep signal generated module could realize the line-by-line pulse width modulation (PWM) driving method, which is valuable for application in high-resolution Micro-LED display compared with the conventional driving method employing global sweep signal. A simple Micro-LED pixel circuit including an inverter module driven by the analog PWM method is employed to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed gate driver. A 10 × 10 green Micro-LED display array has been fabricated with MO-TFTs backplane technology. It is shown that a 10-μs width pulse and linearly scanning sweep signal can be successfully output. Consequently, the pixel circuit array can be driven properly under analog line-by-line PWM driving method.

title: Subjective assessment of visual fidelity: Comparison of forced-choice methods
paper_text:
Abstract
Increases in display resolution, frame rate, and bit depth, particularly with advances in stereoscopic 3D (S3D) displays, have increased demand for efficient compression throughout the imaging pipeline. To meet such requirements, typically the aim is to reduce bandwidth while presenting content that is visually indistinguishable from the original uncompressed versions. Subjective image quality assessment is essential and multiple methods have been proposed. Of these, the ISO/IEC 29170-2 flicker paradigm is a rigorous method used to define visually lossless performance. However, it is possible that the enhanced sensitivity to artifacts in the presence of flicker does not predict visibility under natural viewing conditions. Here, we test this prediction using high-dynamic range S3D images and video under flicker and non-flicker protocols. As hypothesized, sensitivity to artifacts was greater when using the flicker paradigm, but no differences were observed between the non-flicker paradigms. Results were modeled using the Pyramid of Visibility, which predicted artifact detection driven by moderately low spatial frequencies. Overall, our results confirm the flicker paradigm is a conservative estimate of visually lossless behavior; it is highly unlikely to miss artifacts that would be visible under normal viewing. Conversely, artifacts identified by the flicker protocol may not be problematic in practice.

1 INTRODUCTION
Advances in stereoscopic 3D (S3D) augmented and virtual reality displays have been accompanied by an increased demand for more efficient compression to support higher bandwidth across display links. A wide range of compression algorithms exist (e.g., H.264, Intra-only, Motion JPEG 2000, Dirac/VC-2, HEVC, DSC 1.2a and VDC-M v1.2.2) to meet such demands; however, in many cases, the codecs are not visually lossless, that is, visual quality is noticeably degraded following compression.1-4 Objective metrics, including peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), mean squared error (MSE), sCIELAB, HDR-VDP-2, feature similarity index metric (FSIM), and the structural similarity index measure (SSIM),5-8 are often used to quantify the loss of image quality. PSNR and MSE are metrics that quantify absolute error in image representation, while FSIM and SSIM are normalized metrics that are designed to capture saliency-based errors. Additionally, sCIELAB and HDR-VDP-2 are modeled from the human visual system and consider differences in color, spatial information, and luminance. These latter methods have been refined to model important characteristics that drive human performance such as contrast sensitivity and spatial frequency dependence9 or the role of eye movements and attention.10 While these objective methods capture important aspects of signal degradation, they are unable to consistently predict human viewers' sensitivity to compression artifacts and need to be validated and calibrated against human performance.11-13 In the case of PSNR and MSE the failure to predict human performance is likely due to their use of absolute error without consideration for any spatial or temporal differences between images.14 However, even sCIELAB, which incorporates knowledge of variation in human visual contrast sensitivity, there are many instances where the modeled color differences are low, but subjective difference scores are high.15 The HDR-VDP-2 has been shown to effectively predict subjective image quality for some content7; however, human temporal sensitivity is not incorporated, which will result in the omission of temporally based artifacts. Other versions of the VDP metric, for instance, the FovVideoVDP include some general properties of human temporal vision (i.e., sustained vs. transient processing). None of these approaches model the combined spatial and temporal (spatiotemporal) contrast sensitivity of the human visual system. As outlined below, we implement the Pyramid of Visibility,16 a relatively simple model of human spatiotemporal vision that allows for predictions and explanation of artifacts based on well-understood human visual perceptual mechanisms.

1.1 Subjective assessment
Subjective assessment procedures vary depending on the content and the end goal. Rating scales are relatively easy to apply and are often used to evaluate the visual appeal or the degradation of compressed images/video.17 That is, given that they are visible, how bothersome are compression artifacts? However, in some instances, the fidelity of the compressed image is of primary importance. In these cases, assessment methods based on psychophysical procedures are often used.

1.1.1 Rating scales
When using rating scales, viewers are asked to rate quality according to a given paradigm, such as the single-stimulus, double-stimulus, similarity judgment, or forced-choice methodology. The widely used ITU-R BT.500 standard outlines several rating scale-based methods.18 The ratings can be made using either discrete or continuous grading scales. For example, in the Double-Stimulus Impairment Scale, observers are required to rate the quality of a compressed image or sequence relative to a known original reference. The grading scale focuses on the degradation or impairment and is evaluated using a scale ranging from 1 (very annoying) through 5 (imperceptible). Ridder found that image quality assessments using a categorical rating scale depended on whether observers were judging overall image quality or overall image impairment.19 Thus, task-dependent response strategies seem to be important to perceived visual degradation in addition to the objective differences in the images.

1.1.2 Forced-choice procedures
When image fidelity is paramount, as in the case of visually lossless compression, highly sensitive assessment methods are needed. These methods are based on classic psychophysical procedures designed to assess and characterize detection and discrimination thresholds.20 Typically, such methods employ forced-choice tasks in which viewers must choose between two (or more) images, based on the detectability of artifacts or distortions. While these approaches are applied to a wide range of domains in which image quality is relevant, for instance, tone-mapping, here, we focus on the assessment of compression outcomes. In a conventional forced-choice assessment procedure, the reference (original) and comparison (compressed) images are presented in different locations (or at different times), and the viewer must identify, which is compressed. While useful, this approach may underrepresent detection rates because the image distortions may be in unattended image regions (particularly if the images are large). The ISO/IEC international standard 29170-2, Annex A (link), which describes best practices for subjective image quality assessment, outlines one such “normative” forced-choice technique.21 This standard also presents a modified forced-choice task in their Annex B (link) based on work by Hoffman and Stolizka.22 This method uses a flicker or toggle procedure in an effort to help draw attention to regions containing compression artifacts. According to their “flicker paradigm,” images are presented side-by-side (compressed and original), but they both alternate with the original image every 100 ms (5 Hz). If the compressed image is visually lossless, the images in the two locations will appear identical, and performance will be at chance. However, artifacts in the compressed image will appear to flicker, which will result in higher detection rates. Another important feature of this paradigm is that regions of interest are cropped and presented to the viewer, instead of the full-field image. This has two consequences: It restricts attention to a focused region and partially controls for the fact that sensitivity to flicker is higher in the peripheral visual field.23, 24

A version of this standard25 (Annex A, see section C5, ISO, 2020) was also introduced by Stolitzka et al.26 to evaluate moving content. According to this paradigm, the compressed and original images are again presented side-by-side but panned back and forth. Images in the sequence are successively shifted by 1 pixel in both the x- and y-directions, compressed, cropped to a region of interest, and presented. Here, no flicker is introduced; instead, the content moves within the viewing area (rectangular region), which should cause compression artifacts to vary dynamically in the images. In separate blocks of trials, Allison et al.27 used a non-flickering panning protocol and the flicker paradigm to assess the performance of display stream codecs applied to 2D standard dynamic range (SDR) images. Their results showed that when the images were panned, observers were less sensitive to compression artifacts compared to when using the flicker protocol.27 These findings are likely due to a phenomenon known as motion silencing where the global motion of a stimulus can reduce viewers' ability to detect changes within the stimulus.28-30 These types of results speak to the importance of developing effective quality assessment methods that take into consideration the sensitivities of the human visual system, the presentation mode, and the type of content being assessed (e.g., 2D, S3D, video, images).

It has been argued that the flicker protocol is highly sensitive and conservatively defines visually lossless criteria.31 Further, image degradation that would normally go unnoticed may become visible because of the temporal interleaving; under normal viewing conditions original and compressed content are not interleaved or typically presented simultaneously. Another two-alternative forced-choice task that does not rely on temporal alternation is described in the ISO/IEC 29170-2 protocol (Annex A).21 In this case, observers compare two images (one compressed and one original) to a third, original; reference image where they select which of the images was compressed. This simultaneous non-flickering protocol resembles simultaneous rating methods for assessing visual appeal (e.g., ITU-R BT.500, Annex 4 Stimulus-comparison methods), but the goal is quite different. The simultaneous rating methods present the reference as a standard against which to rate image degradation or differences, whereas in the ISO 29170 protocol, the goal is to detect the presence of these differences. While the original and compressed are still presented simultaneously, arguably the non-flickering presentation is more consistent with natural viewing than the flicker paradigm.

The flicker paradigm has been widely used in the assessment of image quality in immersive displays,32 content transmitted or stored in uncompressed form,33 and recent lossless display stream compression algorithms.34, 35 The ISO/IEC 29170-2 flicker protocol21 has recently been adapted for use with S3D imagery as most immersive displays present content stereoscopically. Features inherent to S3D content (e.g., monocular regions from occlusions and specular highlights) and binocular near-eye displays (e.g., radial distortion and chromatic aberration) have been shown to affect perceived image quality but only for particular images.36-38 Therefore, subjective image quality from S3D features may potentially be content-specific. Arguably, to guarantee visually lossless performance for all expected content, whether presented in 2D or 3D, it is important to adopt a strict method to validate compression algorithms. Testolina et al.39 recently compared different subjective assessment protocols and included both non-flicker and flicker paradigms. However, they reported that under their test conditions, it was difficult to compare the flicker paradigm with other protocols as artifact sensitivity was too high to provide meaningful results. Therefore, to date, there has been little work done to compare the flicker paradigm with other forced-choice procedures where observers show both high, moderate, and low artifact sensitivity for different types of images and video. The goal of this study is to bridge this gap, to systematically compare flicker and non-flicker based forced-choice protocols for images and video under lossless compression. To this end we compare three subjective assessment methods (all defined in the ISO/IEC 29170-2 standard) for two display stream codecs (DSC 1.2a and VDC-M v1.2.2) applied to the same HDR S3D images and video. These methods include the flicker protocol, side-by-side non-flickering images, and video sequences.

2 METHODS
2.1 Observers
In total, 104 observers were screened using the Titmus V4 Vision Screener™ for stereoacuity (minimum 40 arc s), visual acuity (at least 20/25 for inclusion), and color vision (Ishihara color slides), and 24 were excluded (seven for stereo, two for color, 14 for acuity, and one for failing to detect compression artifacts in catch trials). All observers were recruited from the York University community and a total of 80 observers participated in the experiment. Two groups of 20 observers were tested in the single frame non-flicker and flicker paradigms, and 40 observers (20 per condition) were tested in the non-flicker video paradigm. Of the 20 observers tested using the non-flicker single frame conditions, nine were female, and 11 were male with a median age of 24.5 years. Of the 20 observers tested using the flicker paradigm, 14 were female, and six were male with a median age of 26.5 years. Of the 40 observers tested in the non-flicker video paradigm, 24 were female, and 16 were male with a median age of 23. The minimum and maximum ages were 19 and 38 years, respectively.

2.2 Apparatus
Testing took place in a test station booth (65″W × 97″H × 98″L) surrounded by floor-length dark gray curtains. The test station contained a mirror stereoscope with dual ASUS PA32UCX HDR monitors (70.8 × 39.8 cm @60 Hz, screen resolution: 3840 × 2160 pixels, color: P3 BT2020 container, 10-bit PQ, peak luminance 1000 cd/m2) viewed through fully silvered mirrors placed at ±45° to the observer's face, to present separate left- and right-eye views for the S3D images (see Figure 1). The monitors were spatially calibrated using a dichoptic grid pattern to ensure horizontal and vertical alignment. The background luminance for both monitors was 0.01 cd/m2. The room lights remained off to control ambient light and to ensure the two monitors of the stereoscope were equally illuminated. The host computer was an Intel i7-9700k, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 GPU, running Windows 10, 64-bit operating system. The distance from the participant's eyes to the display's screens was 63 cm for a display resolution of 60 pixels per degree. A chin rest was used to stabilize the observer's head and position the eyes at the appropriate locations in the stereoscope. Custom C++ code based on DirectXTK HDR was used to display the stimuli and record responses. Observers responded using a Logitech gamepad and Philips SHP1900 over-ear headphones were used to block environmental noise and present auditory feedback following an incorrect response (500 Hz, 0.1 s tone).

2.3 Stimuli
Five custom stereoscopic computer-generated video sequences were used in this study (see thumbnails and link in Figure 2). Each scene was created in Blender and was rendered stereoscopically using off-axis projection with three different types of motion: camera motion (camera trucking movement), object motion (an object moves laterally across the frame), and object rotation (an object rotates about its local vertical axis). Each 8-s sequence was rendered at 30 frames per second (240 frames per eye). All pairs of stereoscopic left-eye and right-eye frames were 4 K resolution (3840 × 2160) HDR at 30 bits per pixel and PQ (SMPTE 2084) encoding. The full frame images were compressed using state-of-the-art lossless compression algorithms (DSC 1.2a and VDC-M v1.2.2 codecs), each with settings of two slices per line and a slice height of 108 pixels. Note that left- and right-eye images were compressed independently, which could potentially result in distinct compression artifacts arising from differences between the stereopair images. Both the full frame (3840 × 2160) original (reference) and compressed images were cropped to 1200 × 1000 pixels. Crop regions were selected based on where compression artifacts were most visible across a given motion sequence, which was determined by two expert observers based on preliminary thorough flicker and video analysis. Objective screening was also performed and consisted of PSNR and sCIELAB40 to observe whether image quality changed across the motion sequence. Additionally, a subset of 60 frames was selected from the original 240 to present when artifacts were most visible in the sequence. The same crop regions were used for all three test paradigms.

In a separate publication,41 we describe a comprehensive subjective video quality assessment with the complete set of conditions described above. A subset of five scene-shot combinations was used in the current study to compare the non-flicker video, single frame non-flicker, and flicker paradigms: (1) creek camera motion at nominal (production standard) compression, (2) desk camera motion at nominal compression, (3) kitchen object motion at an aggressive level of compression (compression ratio higher than the production standard), (4) rain object motion at aggressive compression, and (5) Sushi Camera Motion at aggressive compression.

2.4 Task
A between-subjects design was used with separate groups of observers participating in each of the three test paradigms. The independent variables were the group (corresponding to the paradigms: flicker, single frame non-flicker, and video), and the stimulus content/category compressed with a given codec. The dependent variable was the proportion of correct detections of the compressed content.
Flicker: In the original flicker protocol, observers perform a two-alternative forced-choice task where two images are viewed for 4 s on a single display with ambient environmental illumination. In the current study, observers performed a modified version of the two-alternative forced-choice task described in ISO/IEC 29170-2 (Annex B).21 The modifications consisted of a longer stimulus presentation time (8 s), no environment illumination aside from the displays, and the use of the mirror stereoscope for all test conditions. Consistent with the protocol, test-image, and reference-image sequences were displayed side by side. In the test-image sequences, the compressed version of the image was interleaved with the uncompressed version and in the reference-image sequences the uncompressed version was interleaved with itself (effectively, in the latter case, the uncompressed image is shown continuously). The interleave frequency was 5 Hz (100 ms for each phase of the alternation), which meant that visible image differences between compressed and uncompressed would appear to flicker during the alternation (see Figure 3B). On each trial, observers indicated which of the two images contained flicker.
Single frame non-flicker: Observers performed a two-alternative forced-choice task as described in the ISO/IEC 29170-2 protocol (Annex A).21 The task was to indicate which of two statically presented images (compressed and uncompressed) on the bottom half of the display was different from a third uncompressed reference image located above in the center of the display (see Figure 3A).
Non-flicker video: The observers' task was identical to the single frame, non-flicker group described above, but image sequences (videos) were presented instead of images. Videos were displayed and looped for a total of 8 s, and observers could only respond after the sequence presentation was complete.
In all cases, participants were instructed to choose either the left or right stimulus via button presses on a Logitech controller, making their best guess if unsure. Single images from each of the five scenes shown in Figure 2, and both codecs (DSC1.2a and VDC-M 1.2.2) were viewed by the non-flicker single frame and flicker groups, resulting in a total of 10 conditions, each repeated 20 times. In addition, 40 control/catch trials (two images × 20 trials compressed with highly visible JPEG 2000 compression) were included for a combined total of 240 trials per observer. The trials were divided into four blocks of 60 trials, and each trial was displayed for a total of 8 s, but responses could be made within or after the 8 s.

3 RESULTS
We hypothesized that observers would be more sensitive to compression artifacts when using the flicker paradigm. To test this hypothesis, we fit the data using a generalized linear mixed model (GLMM) following the approach described by Cutone et al.42 GLMM analysis was performed using the R statistical software environment and maximum likelihood estimation was used for fitting data using lme4's “glmer”,43 which is a GLMM function from the linear mixed-effects models library. As expected, all control conditions resulted in performance of at least a 95% detection rate demonstrating attention and understanding of the task. The results from the control conditions were excluded from the analysis as they were unrelated to the experimental hypotheses. To assess the effects of the rendered scene, assessment paradigm, and their possible interaction with artifact visibility, the following model was used:
where pijk is the probability of a correct response for Subject i, Condition j, and measurement k with a random error term, εijk. Coefficients β0ij represents the subject-dependent and condition-dependent intercept, and β1j is the condition-dependent paradigm effect. In the model, paradigm (flicker, single frame non-flicker, and non-flicker video) is a fixed effect, while subject and condition (the combination of the image/video and compression level) were considered as random factors. Responses were either correct or not, so the error distribution of the dependent variable was modeled as binomial. The analysis showed a significant effect of the paradigm on artifact detection (χ2 [2] = 70.151, p < 0.001). The lsmeans package in R was used to obtain the least square mean predictions, and we applied the false detection rate p-value correction at an alpha level of 0.05. Figure 4 shows the results of the comparisons between the flicker, non-flicker single frame, and non-flicker video groups collapsed across the codecs and image/video conditions. Overall, there were significantly higher rates of detection in the flicker paradigm compared to the non-flicker paradigms. No significant difference was observed between the non-flicker single frame and non-flicker video groups.

Descriptive statistics were computed for all observers in each group and plotted using the ISO/IEC 29170-2 recommended format. Figures 5 and 6 show the average proportion correct for each condition with DSC1.2a and VDC-M 1.2.2 compression, respectively. In the ISO/IEC 29170-2 original recommendation, an image or video is considered visually lossless when observers' correct detection rates fall below 75%. A modified recommendation also takes into account the standard deviation.27, 35 In the results reported here, visually lossless is defined as the mean falling below 75%, and visually lossy occurs when the mean is above 75%. It can be seen in Figures 5 and 6 that both codecs generally performed well, and visually lossless behavior was found. Consistent with our overall finding that the flicker paradigm is more sensitive, the few cases where visually lossless behavior was not seen were all obtained with the flicker paradigm and aggressive compression.

4 MODELING COMPRESSION ARTIFACT VISIBILITY
As discussed in the Introduction, several objective metrics are available to quantify the loss of image quality following compression. Some of these metrics incorporate features of the human visual system to characterize how sensitive an observer may be to image degradation, but they do not take into account the spatiotemporal variation in human contrast sensitivity. Given that the flicker paradigm by definition introduces temporal variation, we implemented the Pyramid of Visibility, which models human contrast sensitivity as a linear combination of spatial frequency, temporal frequency, and the log of adapting retinal illumination.44-47 This application of the spatiotemporal contrast sensitivity function in the frequency domain describes the visibility of image features in terms of their spatial and temporal frequency and illumination. Despite the simplicity of the model, it is claimed that it accurately models most of the operating range of human vision and most practical situations of interest. However, given that images in the flicker paradigm alternate at a fixed 5 Hz while the non-flicker paradigm is static at 0 Hz, their respective temporal frequencies allow straightforward use of the Pyramid of Visibility to predict artifact detection.

4.1 Modeling dependence on retinal illuminance
Before implementing the model, image preprocessing was performed to obtain retinal illumination values. The original and compressed cropped images were first converted from RGB color space to grayscale. Images were then converted to luminance space for the display, which was calibrated from luminance measurements taken prior to testing. We presented a rectangular patch on a black background and used a colorimeter to measure luminance directly on the displays at a range of grayscale values. The measurements encompassed the range of luminance values of the tested images and were fit using a generalized linear model, which closely conformed to the expected SMPTE ST 2084 PQ transfer function (see Figure 7), which can represent HDR viewing with luminance values up to 10,000 cd/m2.48

Once transformed to luminance, the images were converted to retinal illumination in Trolands using an online calculator where light-adapted pupil size was determined from each image's mean luminance, angular size, and mean age of the observers.49 Contrast sensitivities were subsequently modeled from the retinal illumination of each image with the Pyramid of Visibility (see Figure 8) using Watson and Ahumada's44 equation:

Pyramid of Visibility models for two example images. Log contrast sensitivity is plotted as a function of spatial and temporal frequency. The sushi scene exhibits lower retinal illuminance compared to the kitchen scene, resulting in a smaller pyramid and lower contrast sensitivity.
The log of contrast sensitivity (S) was calculated as a function of temporal frequency (W), spatial frequency (F), and the log of retinal illuminance (I) where c0 = 2, cW = −0.06, and cI = 0.5. Values for the coefficients in the Pyramid of Visibility were derived from Watson and Ahumada's44 best fitting parameters to existing experimental data. This was done to establish the predicted baseline contrast sensitivity using the uncompressed image as representative for each condition.

4.2 Spatial orientation and frequency filtering
Following conversion from pixel values to illuminance, the original and compressed images were filtered using spatially-oriented Gabor filters at a range of center frequencies:
where λ represents the carrier wavelength, θ represents the orientation, 
 represents the phase, σ represents the Gaussian envelope standard deviation, and γ represents the aspect ratio of the Gaussian window. To maintain a constant bandwidth, σ varied so that it was equal to 0.5λ (see Figure 9). The phase and aspect ratio of each filter were 
= 0, and γ = 1.0. The filters were implemented using the OpenCV library in Python.
Details are in the caption following the image
FIGURE 9
Open in figure viewer
PowerPoint
Gabor filter bank showing all combinations of spatial frequencies (columns) and spatial orientations (rows).
Each scene was filtered using all combinations of four spatial orientations (two diagonal, one horizontal, and one vertical) by five spatial frequencies (1/λ) ranging from 4.0 to 12.0 cycles per degree (cpd). We used spatial frequencies above 3.0 cpd because the Pyramid of Visibility models moderate to high frequencies, while contrast sensitivity at low frequencies does not show the same linear relationship.44 Additionally, the range of spatial frequencies were selected to span the peak of the human contrast sensitivity function. The sampling performed here approximates the effective sampling of spatial frequency and orientation channels in the human visual system.50

4.3 Artifact sensitivity and flicker detection
After filtering the images, we took the difference between the filtered compressed and filtered original images for every combination of spatial orientation and frequency. Contrast maps were then computed at each pixel position by dividing the image difference by the Gabor-filtered original image luminance. Each contrast value was treated as visible if it was greater than that of 1/contrast sensitivity of its respective original image. That is, contrast discrimination can be treated as the number of just-noticeable differences as a function of spatial and temporal frequency.51 The predicted image visibility was obtained from the Pyramid of Visibility (see Figure 8) calculated for the original image's illumination, at the respective spatial frequency, and a temporal frequency of 5 Hz consistent with the flicker alternation rate or 0 Hz consistent with the non-flicker paradigm. The predicted degree of visibility was computed as the peak of visible compression artifacts, from all combinations of spatial frequency and orientation, divided by the Pyramid's modeled sensitivity. That is, we defined how detectable compression artifacts should be as a ratio of the modeled visibility threshold. Pairwise comparisons within each spatial frequency revealed that modeled visibility thresholds, averaged across scenes, were significantly greater for the flicker paradigm compared to the non-flicker paradigm at 4.0 (p = 0.09), 6.0 (p = 0.09), 8.0 (p = 0.016), 10.0 (p = 0.015), and 12.0 cpd (p = 0.013).

Hence, artifact visibility was expected to increase when images were flickered compared to non-flickering presentation, consistent with our experimental results. To evaluate this relationship further, the predicted visibilities were correlated with the mean flicker detection rate for each image. However, to obtain meaningful correlations, a larger and more diverse dataset is required. Therefore, we incorporated subjective results from Mohona et al.,37 which were obtained from 4 K HDR S3D images using the flicker paradigm with the same apparatus used in the current study. This additional modeling is restricted to the flicker paradigm as in this study; we did not use the single frame non-flicker paradigm. The predicted visibility was estimated by selecting the combination of spatial orientation and frequency that produced the highest sensitivity for each image, so selected artifacts were unique to a given image's spatial features. A 1 by 1-degree window was systematically scanned across all areas of each image to identify the region with the highest mean pixel intensity. The window size approximated the size of the foveola, the rod-free area in the retina that supports the highest visual acuity.52 A Pearson correlation was conducted on the combined dataset, comprising images from both the present study and the study by Mohona et al.37 There was a significant positive relationship between predicted artifact visibility and flicker detection, r(88) = 0.57, p < 0.001 (see Figure 10). However, it is important to note that in the “Desk” and “Rain” scenes, the compression artifacts were extremely detectable. This is particularly apparent as the model predicts high visibility for the Desk scene (see Figure 10). Compression artifacts are most visible in the high frequency patterns located in the monitors in this scene. The Rain scene is also distinguished in that it has a noisy, stochastic pattern of raindrops superimposed on the image, which makes it harder to compress but the position and shape of individual raindrops is perceptually unimportant. For comparison, we also conducted correlations with the SSIM and sCIELAB metrics with flicker detection rates from the present study and Mohona et al.37 The SSIM values were 0.9999 for every image, indicating no discernable relationship with flicker detection. Note that, since 1.0 is a perfect SSIM score, which indicates zero loss in image quality, this outcome is likely due to the use of lossless codecs. The sCIELAB metric produced a nonsignificant correlation strength of R = 0.29, which may be partially explained by the metric not taking into account temporal frequency information. Overall, our analysis shows that artifact visibility is reasonably well predicted using the Pyramid of Visibility for these lossless compression algorithms.

5 DISCUSSION
We compared the performance of different subjective assessment paradigms for detection of artifacts in HDR S3D images and video. It has been argued that the flicker paradigm is an extremely conservative assessment method; if so, it should result in higher detection rates compared to the single frame non-flicker and non-flicker video conditions.31 We tested state-of-the-art visually lossless display stream compression algorithms. As expected, when used at their designed (nominal) and even beyond (aggressive) compression levels, we found visually lossless performance for all paradigms. The sole exception was the desk camera motion scene that contained visible compression artifacts at aggressive compression levels when flickered but not when presented statically. Moderately higher rates of detection were also found in the rain object motion and kitchen object motion scenes when flickered but not high enough to be categorized as visually lossy. These findings are consistent with our hypothesis and demonstrate that flicker testing is more sensitive as it can identify artifacts that are not visible in non-flickering image or video testing.

Image differences were likely more difficult to detect in the non-flicker methods compared to the flicker paradigm because the sizes of the distorted regions were small and the luminance and color differences minimal. Any image degradation between the original and compressed content is typically localized to specific areas and often appears well integrated with the remaining parts of the image that are not degraded. As predicted, alternating the compressed and original images creates a temporal difference signal that does not exist in the compressed image alone. This temporal alternation brings the artifact closer to the peak of the contrast sensitivity function making it more visible,23, 31 while non-flicker methods with a 0 Hz signal are unchanging and do not show the same visibility. Another factor is that the image regions to be compared are overlaid in the flicker case and do not require eye movements. Thus while artifacts were not visible for non-flickering conditions in both codecs (see Figure 6) for every image, they became more visible when flickered in some cases. Daly outlines that peak temporal sensitivity required for contrast discrimination is roughly near 3 Hz, while frequencies close to 0 Hz show much lower sensitivity; the flicker paradigm falls well-within this peak region, which is not the case for the non-flicker paradigms, which are at 0Hz.47, 53 This is also reflected in the Pyramid of Visibility estimates of sensitivity to compression artifacts in flickering versus non-flickering images. That is, artifacts should be more detectable under the flicker paradigm given that contrast sensitivity is higher at a temporal alternation rate of 5 Hz (relative to 0 Hz).44

Apart from the presence of motion in the video sequence, the arrangement and task in the two non-flicker paradigms were very similar (no alternation with the original image and a three cropped-image arrangement). We expected that artifacts would be less visible in the non-flicker video relative to the single-frame non-flicker condition. However, no significant difference was found between non-flickering image and video conditions. The subtle nature of the artifacts in both paradigms was likely responsible, resulting in a floor effect. Compression artifacts were difficult to detect for the majority of image/video conditions even when flickered, so observers were at or near chance performance in the remaining conditions. Another factor that may have contributed to the generally low artifact detection rates is the fact that the stimuli were presented in S3D. There is some evidence that artifacts are less visible in S3D, relative to 2D.36 However, these findings are also content-specific; the saliency of a compression artifact and its location in an image may depend on the stimulus being presented.38 At this point, we do not know if or how the presentation mode (flicker vs. non-flicker vs. video) interacts with the location of the artifacts in depth.

Observers were more sensitive to compression artifacts in the flicker group compared to the non-flicker video group, which is consistent with the results of Allison et al.27 where sensitivity was decreased when images were panned instead of flickered. The main difference between the moving stimuli in that study and this one is that the video stimuli presented here were not panned, but rather, a rendered sequence of changing successive frames was presented. This meant that image degradation from compression was not equal across the sequence of frames, and therefore, some artifacts appeared briefly and only at a single point in time. Thus, while the current results are consistent with the global motion of a scene masking observers' sensitivity to compression artifacts, that is, motion silencing, they may also reflect the transient nature of the artifacts.

6 CONCLUSION
In sum, our results confirm the utility of the flicker paradigm for evaluation of image and video quality following lossless compression. Although images are not typically flickered under natural viewing situations, this paradigm is an efficient proxy for video testing. Further, our preliminary modeling suggests that objective evaluation methods based on human visual sensitivity show promise in anticipating artifact sensitivity using the flicker paradigm. The Pyramid of Visibility's use of both spatial and temporal contrast sensitivity allows us to capture differences in artifact visibility between flicker and non-flicker paradigms. This is evidenced by the relatively poorer correlations from metrics that do not model temporal frequency (see Section 4.3). We have confirmed that this paradigm is highly sensitive; artifact detection rates were higher in this condition compared to the single frame non-flicker and non-flicker video paradigms. Flicker testing provides a convenient means of validating the visually lossless performance of an image processing algorithm under defined viewing conditions and content. It errs on the side of being too sensitive and is unlikely to miss artifacts that would be visible under normal viewing. The flip side of this high sensitivity is that flicker testing results should not be used to predict the visibility of individual distortions in specific images or sequences.

title: 4670-PPI OLEDoS pixel circuit design for wide data voltage range in a 5 V 0.13 μm CMOS process
paper_keyword: current uniformity, microdisplay, OLEDoS, pixel circuit, wide data voltage rage
paper_text:
Abstract
OLEDoS displays hold great promise for AR/VR applications, but the ultra-high pixel density required for microdisplays, exceeding several thousand PPI, presents significant challenges for pixel circuit design. Above all, the maximum OLED current of each OLEDoS sub-pixel is extremely low, on the order of several nanoamperes or less, resulting in a data voltage range narrower than a few hundred millivolts. Consequently, for example, when expressing 10-bit grayscale, designing data driver circuits to divide the data voltage range into 1024 levels becomes exceedingly challenging. Furthermore, circuit techniques that compensate for variations in transistor characteristics are nearly inapplicable due to the ultra-small pixel area. This paper proposes a pixel circuit design method to expand the data voltage range for a 4670-PPI OLEDoS display while considering current uniformity. The effectiveness of the proposed pixel circuit was verified through measurements on 40 × 120 pixel arrays fabricated in a 5 V 0.13 μm CMOS process.
